title: Methods and devices using individually addressable microgel electrophoresis lane synthesis for bioassays
abstract: In certain embodiments, an individually addressable microgel electrophoresis lane (ÂµGEL) is provided that includes a single or plurality of micron-sized well(s) surrounded by a hydrogel frame. A micron-sized well serves as a housing for decomposable biological entities for single-cell bioassays and other applications.
claims:
Claims CLAIMS What is claimed is: 1. A substantially planar array of microgel electrophoresis structures, said array comprising: a plurality of microgel electrophoresis structures (pGel(s)) wherein each microgel electrophoresis structure comprises: a free-standing polymeric gel that forms said microgel electrophoresis structure where said polymeric gel is a gel compatible with electrophoretic separation or isolation of one or more analytes and configured to separate a sample along a directional axis; and one or more sample receiving wells disposed in said microgel electrophoresis structure; and a separation zone delineating each microgel electrophoresis structure perimeter where said separation zone is structured to facilitate separation of the microgel electrophoresis structures from each other. 2. The array of microgel electrophoresis structures of claim 1, wherein said separation zone is structured to facilitate recovery of at least 50%, or at least 60%, or at least 70% , or at least 80% , or at least 90% , or at least 95% of the pGels comprising the array. 3. The array of microgel electrophoresis structures according to any one of claims 1-2, wherein said separation zone is structured to facilitate separation of the microgel electrophoresis structures from each other while retaining sufficient physical integrity to permit interrogation of separated analyte(s) in the released microgel electrophoresis structures. 4. The array of microgel electrophoresis structures according to any one of claims 1-3, wherein said separation zone comprises troughs around each microgel electrophoresis structure. 5. The array of microgel electrophoresis structures of claim 4, wherein said troughs comprise a trough floor formed from the same polymeric gel material that forms the microgel electrophoresis structures. 6. The array of microgel electrophoresis structures of claim 4, wherein said troughs comprise a trough floor formed from a different polymeric material than the material that forms the microgel electrophoresis structures. 7. The array of microgel electrophoresis structures according to any one of claims 4-6, wherein said troughs comprise a trough floor that is sufficiently thin to facilitate mechanical separation of the microgel electrophoresis from each other by mechanical pulling or shearing. 8. The array of microgel electrophoresis structures according to any one of claims 1-7, wherein said separation zone comprises a plurality of perforations that facilitate separation of the microgel electrophoresis structures from each other. 9. The array of microgel electrophoresis structures according to any one of claims 1-8, wherein said separation zone comprises one or more scoring lines that facilitate separation of the microgel electrophoresis structures from each other. 10. The array of microgel electrophoresis structures according to any one of claims 1-9, wherein said separation zone comprises a material comprising a photocleavable linker that facilitates degradation of the trough when exposed to a wavelength of light that cleaves said photocleavable linker. 11. The array of microgel electrophoresis structures according to any one of claims 1-9, wherein said separation zone comprises a material that is hydrolyzed by a reagent that does not hydrolyze the microgel electrophoresis structures. 12. The array of microgel electrophoresis structures according to any one of claims 1-9, wherein said separation zone comprises a material that preferentially absorbs laser radiation as compared to the microgel electrophoresis structures and degrades when exposed to said laser radiation. 13. The array of microgel electrophoresis structures according to any one of claims 1-12, wherein said array comprise at least 500, or at least 1,000, or at least 1,500, or at least 2,000, or at least 2,500, or at least 3,000, or at least 3,500, or at least 4,000, or at least 5,000 microgel electrophoresis structures. 14. The array of microgel electrophoresis structures according to any one of claims 1-13, wherein said microgel electrophoresis structures are disposed within a surface 

 area of 20 cm 2 or less, or 15 cm 2 or less or 10 cm 2 or less, or 5 cm 2 or less, or 3 cm 2 or less or 2 cm 2 or less or 1 cm 2 or less. 15. The array of microgel electrophoresis structures according to any one of claims 1-14, wherein each of said microgel electrophoresis structures range in volume from about 10 pL, or from about 50 pL, or from about 100 pL, or from about 250 pL, or from about 500 pL, or from about 1000 pL up to 10,000 pL, or up to about 5,000 pL, or up to about 4,000 pL, or up to about 3,000 pL, or up to about 2000 pL. 16. The array of microgel electrophoresis structures according to any one of claims 1-15, wherein each of said microgel electrophoresis structures has an area ranging from about from about 1,000 pm 2 , or from about 5,000 pm 2 , or from about 10,000 pm 2 , or from about 20,000 pm 2 , or from about 30,000 pm 2 , or from about 40,000 pm 2 up to about 500,000 pm 2 , or up to about 250,000 pm 2 , or up to about 100,000 pm 2 , or up to about 80,000 pm 2 , or up to about 60,000 pm 2 . 17. The array of microgel electrophoresis structures according to any one of claims 1-16, wherein said microgel each microgel electrophoresis structure is substantially rectangular. 18. The array of microgel electrophoresis structures of claim 17, wherein: said microgel electrophoresis structures each have a length ranging from about 500 pm, or from about 600 pm, or from about 700 pm, or from about 800 pm, or from about 900 pm up to about 3,000 pm, or up to about 2,500 pm, or up to about 2,000 pm, or up to about 1,500 pm, or up to about 1,000 pm; and/or said microgel electrophoresis structures each have a width ranging from about 5 pm, or from about 10 pm, or from about 15 pm, or from about 20 pm, or from about 30 pm, or from about 40 pm up to about 500 pm, or up to about 250 pm, or up to about 200 pm, or up to about 150 pm, or up to about 100 pm, or up to about 80 pm, or up to about 60 pm. 19. The array of microgel electrophoresis structures of claim 18, wherein said microgel electrophoresis structures each have a length of about 950 pm. 20. The array of microgel electrophoresis structures according to any one of claims 18-19, wherein said microgel electrophoresis structures each have a width of about 50 pm. 21. The array of microgel electrophoresis structures according to any one of claims 1-20, wherein said microgel electrophoresis structures each have a thickness (depth) ranging from about from about 15 pm, or from about 20 pm, or from about 30 pm, or from about 40 pm up to about 200 pm, or up to about 150 pm, or up to about 100 pm, or up to about 80 pm, or up to about 60 pm, or up to about 50 pm. 22. The array of microgel electrophoresis structures of claim 21, wherein said microgel electrophoresis structures each have a thickness of about 40 pm. 23. The array of microgel electrophoresis structures according to any one of claims 1-22, wherein said sample receiving wells range in diameter from about 10 pm, or from about 20 pm, or from about 30 pm up to about 100 pm, or up to about up to about 80 pm, or up to about 70 pm, or up to about 60 pm, or up to about 50 pm. 24. The array of microgel electrophoresis structures of claim 23, wherein said sample receiving wells have a diameter of about 30 pm. 25. The array of microgel electrophoresis structures according to any one of claims 1-24, wherein said sample receiving wells are sized to each contain a single cell. 26. The array of microgel electrophoresis structures claim 25, wherein said sample wells are sized to contain a single vertebrate cell. 27. The array of microgel electrophoresis structures according to any one of claims 1-26, wherein said sample receiving wells each have a depth ranging from about from about 15 pm, or from about 20 pm, or from about 30 pm, or from about 40 pm up to about 200 pm, or up to about 150 pm, or up to about 100 pm, or up to about 80 pm, or up to about 60 pm, or up to about 50 pm. 28. The array of microgel electrophoresis structures of claim 27, wherein said sample wells each have a depth of about 40 pm. 29. The array of microgel electrophoresis structures according to any one of claims 1-28, wherein said polymeric gel comprises a hydrogel. 30. The array of microgel electrophoresis structures of claim 29, wherein said polymeric gel comprises a material selected from the group consisting of acrylamide, Ethyl Acrylate, Ethyl Methacrylate, Ethyleneglycol Dimethacrylate, Hydroxy ethyl Acrylate, 

 Isobutyl Methacrylate, Lauryl Methacrylate, Methacrylic Acid, Methyl Acrylate, Methyl Methacrylate, N,N-methylene-bisacryl-amide, n-Butyl Methacrylate, pentaerythritol tetraacrylate, pentaerythritol triacrylate, Pentaerythritol Triacrylate, Polyethylene Glycol (200) Diacrylate, Phenyl acrylate, polyethylene glycol diacrylate), polypropylene diacrylate), l,3-Butanediol Dimethacrylate, l,4-Butanediol Dimethacrylate, l,6-Hexanediol Dimethacrylate, 2,2,3,3,4,4,5,5-Octafluoropenty Acrylate, 2.2.2-Trifluoroethyl 2- methylacrylate, 2-Ethyl Hexyl Acrylate, 2-Hydroxy ethyl Methacrylate, 2-Hydroxypropyl Acrylate, 4-Hydroxybutyl Acrylate, agarose, Allyl Methacrylate, Benzyl Methylacrylate, Butyl Acrylate, collagen type I, collagen type IV., Diethyleneglycol Diacrylate, Diethyleneglycol Dimethacrylate, dimethacrylate, Divinyl benzene, divinyl benzene (DVB), Monoethylene Glycol, poly(ethylene glycol), polypropylene glycol), Polyethylene Glycol (200) Dimethacrylate, Polyethylene Glycol (400) Diacrylate, Polyethylene Glycol (400) Dimethacrylate, Polyethylene Glycol (600) Diacrylate, Polyethylene Glycol (600) Dimethacrylate, Stearyl Methacrylate, Triethylene Glycol, Triethylene Glycol Dimethacrylate, Trimethylolpropane Triacrylate, gelatin, and the like, and mixtures or copolymers thereof. 31. The array of microgel electrophoresis structures of claim 30, wherein said polymeric gel comprises acrylamide. 32. The array of microgel electrophoresis structures of claim 31, wherein said polymeric gel comprises 7% up to 14% acrylamide. 33. A method of separating and detecting one or more analytes in a sample, said method comprising: providing an array of microgel electrophoresis structures according to any one of claims 1-32 with said sample disposed in one or more of said sample receiving wells; placing said array in an electric field where said electric field is sufficient to cause migration of said one or more analytes from the sample receiving wells into and at least partially through the polymeric gel that forms said microgel electrophoresis structure(s); contacting the microgel electrophoresis structures with a reagent comprising a detectable label where the reagent associates with one or more of said analytes; and 

 detecting and/or localizing said detectable label in said microgel electrophoresis structures. 34. The method of claim 33, wherein said sample comprises a sample selected from the group consisting of a cell, a tissue sample, a biological fluid, and an environmental sample. 35. The method of claim 34, wherein said sample comprises a cell. 36. The method of claim 35, wherein said sample comprises a mammalian cell. 37. The method according to any one of claims 34-36, wherein said sample comprises a single cell disposed in each loaded sample receiving well. 38. The method of claim 34, wherein said sample comprises a biological fluid, a cellular extract or digest, or a tissue extract or digest disposed in each loaded sample receiving well. 39. The method according to any one of claims 33-38, wherein said placing said array in an electric field comprises placing said array in an electrophoresis apparatus. 40. The method according to any one of claims 33-39, wherein said electric field is produced by a voltage difference ranging from about 10 V cm 1 , or from about 20 V cm 1 , or from about 30 V cm 1 , or from about 40 V cm 1 up to about 100 V cm 1 , or up to about 90 V cm l, or up to about 80 V cm 1 , or up to about 70 V cm 1 , or up to about 60 V cm 1 41. The method of claim 40, wherein said electric filed is produced by a voltage difference of about 40 V cm 1 . 42. The method according to any one of claims 33-41, wherein said electric field is substantially uniform across said array. 43. The method according to any one of claims 33-42, wherein said detecting comprises using a device selected from the group consisting of a photomultiplier tube, a charge-coupled device, an intensified charge coupled device, a complementary metal- 

 oxide- semi conductor sensor, visual colorimetric readout, a photodiode, and an imaging microscope system. 44. The method according to any one of claims 33-43, wherein said detectable label is selected from the group consisting of a fluorescent label, a colorimetric label, a chemiluminescent label, an enzymatic label comprising an enzyme substrate or an enzyme, a radiolabel, silver particles, gold particles, and a magnetic label. 45. The method according to any one of claims 33-44, wherein said method comprises dehydrating the microgel electrophoresis structures to concentrate the signal produced by the detectable label. 46. The method according to any one of claims 33-45, wherein detecting comprises detecting the analyte in a substantially intact planar array. 47. The method of claim 46, wherein said detecting comprise visualizing the array with an image analysis system. 48. The method according to any one of claims 33-45, wherein detecting comprises detecting the analyte(s) after the microgel electrophoresis structures (pGel(s)) are separated from each other. 49. The method of claim 48, wherein said detecting comprises using flow cytometry to detect the detectable labels associated with separated microgel electrophoresis structures. 50. The method according to any one of claims 33-49, wherein said one or more analytes comprise an analyte selected from the group consisting of a peptide, a nucleic acid, a virus or virus fragment, a bacterium or bacterial fragment, a cytokine, a toxin, an antibody, a peptide nucleic acid, an aptamer, a lectin, an antigen, an enzyme, a small organic molecule, and a cellular receptor binding protein. 51. The method of claim 50, wherein said analyte comprises a protein. 52. The method according to any one of claims 33-51, wherein said method comprises immobilizing the analyte(s) in the microgel electrophoresis structures after the analytes have migrated into the polymeric gel. 53. The method of claim 52, wherein said immobilizing is by application of UV light and/or by chemical cross-linking. 54. The method according to any one of claims 33-53, wherein said contacting and detecting comprises contacting said sample with a primary antibody that binds to an analyte that is to be detected where the primary antibody is attached to said detectable label. 55. The method according to any one of claims 33-53, wherein said contacting and detecting comprises contacting said sample with a primary antibody that binds to an analyte that is to be detecting and contacting said primary antibody with a secondary antibody that binds to said primary antibody where said secondary antibody is attached to said detectable label. 56. The method according to any one of claims 33-55, wherein said method comprises a western blot. 57. The method according to any one of claims 33-55, wherein said method comprises a differential detergent fractionation. 58. The method according to any one of claims 33-50, and 54-55, wherein said analyte comprises a nucleic acid. 59. The wherein said detecting comprises detecting contacting said nucleic acid with an oligonucleotide probe that hybridizes to said nucleic acid where said oligonucleotide probe is attached to said detectable label. 60. A method of making a substantially planar array of microgel electrophoresis structures, said method comprising: providing a mold surface patterned with features that define the shapes and size a pGel array; loading the mold surface with precursors of a polymeric gel; covering the loaded gel with a substantially planar surface; polymerizing the precursors to form a polymerized polymer gel; and releasing the polymerized polymer gel from the mold surface to provide a pGel array disposed on said substantially planar surface. 61. The method of claim 60, wherein said mold surface is configured to pattern the features of a pGel array according to any one of claims 1-29. 62. The method according to any one of claims 60-61, wherein said mold surface comprises an SU-8 surface. 63. The method according to any one of claims 60-62, wherein said substantially planar surface comprises a glass surface. 64. The method of claim 63, wherein said substantially planar surface comprises a glass slide. 65. The method according to any one of claims 63-64, wherein said substantially planar surface is silanized. 66. The method according to any one of claims 60-65, wherein said polymerization comprises photopolymerization by exposing said precursors to a light source capable of inducing polymerization. 67. The method of claim 66, wherein light source comprises a UV light source. 68. The method according to any one of claims 60-65, wherein said polymerization comprises chemical photopolymerization. 69. The method according to any one of claims 60-68, wherein said precursors comprises a material selected from the group consisting of acrylamide, Ethyl Acrylate, Ethyl Methacrylate, Ethyleneglycol Dimethacrylate, Hydroxy ethyl Acrylate, Isobutyl Methacrylate, Lauryl Methacrylate, Methacrylic Acid, Methyl Acrylate, Methyl Methacrylate, N,N-methylene-bisacryl-amide, n-Butyl Methacrylate, pentaerythritol tetraacrylate, pentaerythritol triacrylate, Pentaerythritol Triacrylate, Polyethylene Glycol (200) Diacrylate, Phenyl acrylate, polyethylene glycol diacrylate), polypropylene diacrylate), l,3-Butanediol Dimethacrylate, l,4-Butanediol Dimethacrylate, l,6-Hexanediol Dimethacrylate, 2,2,3,3,4,4,5,5-Octafluoropenty Acrylate, 2.2.2-Trifluoroethyl 2- methylacrylate, 2-Ethyl Hexyl Acrylate, 2-Hydroxy ethyl Methacrylate, 2-Hydroxypropyl Acrylate, 4-Hydroxybutyl Acrylate, agarose, Allyl Methacrylate, Benzyl Methylacrylate, Butyl Acrylate, collagen type I, collagen type IV., Diethyleneglycol Diacrylate, Diethyleneglycol Dimethacrylate, dimethacrylate, Divinyl benzene, divinyl benzene (DVB), 

 Monoethylene Glycol, poly(ethylene glycol), poly(propylene glycol), Polyethylene Glycol (200) Dimethacrylate, Polyethylene Glycol (400) Diacrylate, Polyethylene Glycol (400) Dimethacrylate, Polyethylene Glycol (600) Diacrylate, Polyethylene Glycol (600) Dimethacrylate, Stearyl Methacrylate, Triethylene Glycol, Triethylene Glycol Dimethacrylate, Trimethylolpropane Triacrylate, gelatin, and the like, and mixtures or copolymers thereof. 70. The method of claims 69, wherein said precursors comprise precursors for an acrylamide gel. 71. The method of claim 70, wherein said precursors comprise N,N,N',N'- tetramethylethylenediamine (TEMED) as a catalyst. 72. The method according to any one of claims 70-71, wherein said precursors comprise ammonium persulfate (APS) as an initiator. 73. The method according to any one of claims 60-72, wherein the microgel array is incubated in distilled or deionized water prior to releasing the planar substrate from the mold.
